source_pr.title: ddl: fix flaky test TestGlobalVariablesOnFlashback
source_pr.body:
<!--

Thank you for contributing to TiDB!

PR Title Format:

2. *: what's changed

-->

### What problem does this PR solve?
<!--

Please create an issue first to describe the problem.

There MUST be one line starting with "Issue Number:  " and 
linking the relevant issues via the "close" or "ref".

For more info, check https://pingcap.github.io/tidb-dev-guide/contribute-to-tidb/contribute-code.html#referring-to-an-issue.

-->

Issue Number: close #40287 

Problem Summary:

### What is changed and how it works?

### Check List

Tests <!-- At least one of them must be included. -->

- [ ] Unit test
- [x] Integration test
- [ ] Manual test (add detailed scripts or steps below)
- [ ] No code

Side effects

- [ ] Performance regression: Consumes more CPU
- [ ] Performance regression: Consumes more Memory
- [ ] Breaking backward compatibility

Documentation

- [ ] Affects user behaviors
- [ ] Contains syntax changes
- [ ] Contains variable changes
- [ ] Contains experimental features
- [ ] Changes MySQL compatibility

### Release note

<!-- compatibility change, improvement, bugfix, and new feature need a release note -->

Please refer to [Release Notes Language Style Guide](https://pingcap.github.io/tidb-dev-guide/contribute-to-tidb/release-notes-style-guide.html) to write a quality release note.

```release-note
None
```

symptoms: Flashback-related tests (including `TestGlobalVariablesOnFlashback`) intermittently fail when they obtain an oracle timestamp immediately after DDL/setup and then use it as a flashback target; the timestamp can be too close to the preceding operations.
failure_signature: Flaky flashback tests due to timing dependence on oracle TSO (mitigated by inserting a short `time.Sleep` before `GetTimestamp`).
root_cause_explanation: These flashback tests depend on the relative ordering of operations and the oracle timestamp used as the flashback point. When the test runs quickly, calling `GetTimestamp` immediately after setup/DDL can produce a timestamp that is too close to the preceding operations, leading to timing-dependent behavior. The fix inserts a small `time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)` before fetching the timestamp so the flashback point is safely after the prior actions.
fix_pattern: Add a short `time.Sleep` before fetching the oracle timestamp in flashback-related tests to avoid timing-dependent TSO edge cases.

evidence: evidence: ddl: fix flaky test TestGlobalVariablesOnFlashback
